one_line_takeaway: A GPT-4o-annotated, 249K-query multilingual dataset and benchmark for detecting user-related privacy leakage in real-name LLM chats, where fine-tuned small local models beat prompted 72B models yet still fall short (top info-summarization F1 only ~44.7%) of practical deployment.
key_finding: Fine-tuning small local models on their dataset substantially beats prompting much larger models, but absolute performance on the harder tasks is still far from deployable. The best model, fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B, reaches 87.56% leakage-classification accuracy (Chinese), 74.27% phrase-extraction F1 (Chinese; 65.11% English), and only 44.70% information-summarization F1 (Chinese; 44.7% is the top info-F1). A fine-tuned 1B model outperforms the directly-prompted 72B model (by 7.03% EN / 5.57% ZH on phrase F1). The annotation pipeline itself, with filtering rules, achieves 93.70% phrase F1 vs 74.80% for directly prompting GPT-4o (a +18.90 F1 gain). Traditional PII tools fail badly here — Presidio scores only ~17.6–20.9% phrase F1 vs 65–74% for the SFT model — evidencing that LLM-interaction privacy differs markedly from document PII.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new problem framing — privacy detection for "real-name" user interactions with cloud LLMs, where a user is authenticated and any newly disclosed personal information (not just classic PII) leaks to an honest-but-curious provider. Its own three contributions are: (1) an automated four-step GPT-4o annotation pipeline (leakage classification → extensive privacy-category extraction → phrase extraction with user-relatedness/explicit-reference filtering rules → per-phrase information summarization); (2) the resulting first large-scale multilingual dataset (249,683 queries from 33K dialogues, 154,230 annotated privacy phrases with summarized information, EN+ZH) built from ShareGPT, CrossWOZ, DuConv, and LCCC-base; and (3) a hierarchical evaluation suite (query/phrase/information levels) plus a benchmark of light-weight, locally-deployable LLM baselines. Note: Presidio, MemoAnalyzer, and PAPILLON are cited comparison/related methods, NOT this paper's contributions.
threat_model: Honest-but-curious cloud LLM service provider: users authenticate with real, unique identifiers before interacting, so queries are linked to a known person. The provider does not launch active attacks but may passively infer private, user-related information (preferences, intentions, opinions, status, and other personal details beyond classic PII) from query text. The defense is a light-weight privacy-detection model deployed locally on the user's device (or an org-side proxy) that flags leaking queries, extracts the responsible phrases, and summarizes the leaked information before the query is uploaded, letting the user rewrite or consent. Privacy is scoped to the current user and close associates; disclosures about unrelated third parties are not counted as leakage.
limitations: The "ground truth" is itself GPT-4o-generated, so the dataset inherits any biases/errors of the annotating model (mitigated but not eliminated by human spot-checks of 0.93–0.96 agreement). The best information-summarization F1 is only ~44.7%, which the authors explicitly call insufficient for practical deployment. Evaluation relies on ROUGE-L-based matching (phrase match threshold 0.5), which may over- or under-credit semantic equivalence. Chinese source corpora are human-to-human dialogues repurposed by designating the first speaker as the "user," a heuristic that may not reflect true LLM interaction distributions. Only instruction-tuned Llama-3.2 and Qwen2.5 families are benchmarked. The paper studies detection/alerting, not query rewriting or end-to-end protection.

datasets_used: Own constructed dataset: 249,683 user queries from ~33K dialogues, 154,230 annotated privacy phrases with summarized information (85,320 English + 68,910 Chinese)
LCCC-base (Chinese source corpus); Chinese portion 151,988 queries, 68,910 phrases
quantitative_results: Best model fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B: 87.56% leakage-classification accuracy (Chinese), 83.57% (English)
Phrase-extraction F1: 74.27% Chinese / 65.11% English (SFT Qwen2.5-7B, Table 4)
Information-summarization F1: 44.7% top (Figure 6); Discussion cites 44.70% Chinese
Annotation pipeline phrase F1: 93.70% with filtering rules vs 74.80% for direct GPT-4o prompting (+18.90 F1; Table 2)
Fine-tuned 1B model beats prompted 72B by 7.03% (EN) / 5.57% (ZH) phrase F1
Fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B beats Qwen2.5-72B ICL by 13.68% (EN) / 13.25% (ZH) phrase F1
Traditional PII (Presidio) phrase F1 only 17.58% EN / 20.89% ZH vs 65.11% / 74.27% for SFT Qwen2.5-7B (Table 5)
Human-annotation agreement: leakage classification accuracy 0.96 (recall 0.97, precision 0.94); info-quality score 0.93
Category extraction yielded 325 English and 149 Chinese privacy categories
baselines_compared: Directly prompting GPT-4o (MemoAnalyzer-style) for pipeline comparison
Presidio (spaCy default and transformer-based NER) traditional PII detection
